unit_groups.name: Adv Poly Method
unit_groups.subject: Combinatorics (Gryffindor)
unit_groups.description: Formerly, this was a unit on using combinatorial nullstellensatz, mostly for fun. It was later expanded to additionally include uses of generating functions and related polynomial methods on 3/6 level problems, broadening the scope significantly.

unit_groups.name: AIME Geo
unit_groups.subject: Geometry (Slytherin)
unit_groups.description: Computational geometry problems, many taken from the tail end of the AIME. At the border of computational contests and olympiads.

unit_groups.name: Alg Manip
unit_groups.subject: Algebra (Hufflepuff)
unit_groups.description: Problems that purely involve algebraic manipulations without some other context, e.g. solving rather arbitrary equations or systems of equations.

unit_groups.name: Analysis
unit_groups.subject: Algebra (Hufflepuff)
unit_groups.description: Problems involving some real analysis. This is a pretty technical lecture and will delve into the nuances of converge issues, absolute versus conditional convergence, using calculus properly, compact sets, and so on. Featuring the art of Lagrange multipliers.

unit_groups.name: Analytic NT
unit_groups.subject: Number Theory (Ravenclaw)
unit_groups.description: Problems that involve asymptotic calculations in number theory, featuring some multiplicative number theory. Convolution method, and generally problems that require more technical estimates.

unit_groups.name: Anti Problems
unit_groups.subject: Miscellaneous
unit_groups.description: A unit consisting entirely of troll "anti-problems" which are suitable for giving to your enemies.

unit_groups.name: Arrows
unit_groups.subject: Combinatorics (Gryffindor)
unit_groups.description: Some selected problems revolving around the idea that a function from a set to itself can be thought of as a directed graph with all outdegrees equal to 1. In particular, iterating such a function often involves looking at its cycle decomposition.

unit_groups.name: Art School
unit_groups.subject: Geometry (Slytherin)
unit_groups.description: This is a unit about building "diagram intuition": being able to take a geometry diagram (which may be good or bad) and trying to get a sense of which claims should or shouldn't be true. This is definitely one of the longer geometry units.

unit_groups.name: Bary
unit_groups.subject: Geometry (Slytherin)
unit_groups.description: Barycentric coordinates in olympiad geometry. A follow-up to Chapter 7 of EGMO.

unit_groups.name: C8 Summit
unit_groups.subject: Combinatorics (Gryffindor)
unit_groups.description: A challenging combinatorics unit to conclude the year, with difficult problems reviewing everything that has appeared earlier.

unit_groups.name: Classical Geo
unit_groups.subject: Geometry (Slytherin)
unit_groups.description: Formerly part of "American Geo". This is somewhere between Config Geo and Elem Geo. A lot of these problems involve figuring out what certain points are, adding in new points that were not that already, and altogether slowly piecing together a master diagram that reveals the depth of a certain picture.

unit_groups.name: Collinia Colosseum (Lv 75)
unit_groups.subject: Secret
unit_groups.description: Secret unit that appears at level 75.

unit_groups.name: Complex Nums
unit_groups.subject: Geometry (Slytherin)
unit_groups.description: Complex numbers in olympiad geometry. A follow-up to Chapter 6 of EGMO.

unit_groups.name: Computational Combo
unit_groups.subject: Combinatorics (Gryffindor)
unit_groups.description: The "ARML combo" unit, this counting unit represents the centroid of Rigid, Induct, Formulas, and Grinding. Also contains several black-magic bijections.

unit_groups.name: Config Geo
unit_groups.subject: Geometry (Slytherin)
unit_groups.description: Formerly known as "American Geo". This is a unit on geometry problems with a highly traditional or synthetic flavor, for example USAMO 2016/3 and USAMO 2017/3. These sorts of problems were popular on the USA olympiads and team selection tests around 2016 and it is totally not my fault. These particular ones tend to use common or standard configurations as a base and build on top of them, as opposed to starting afresh.

unit_groups.name: Constructor
unit_groups.subject: Geometry (Slytherin)
unit_groups.description: A mostly-for-fun unit on problems involving constructions. In addition to classical ruler-compass constructions, you may get to use exotic tools that you've never heard of! Includes double-sided rulers, marked rulers, cyclos, circumcircle compasses, splitters, perpendicular bisector rulers, isogonal conjugate finders, and more.

unit_groups.name: Courage
unit_groups.subject: Miscellaneous
unit_groups.description: A difficult unit consisting of problems with deceptively short statements. A lot of the difficulty of these problems is setting up an entire framework to attack a simply stated problem. These setups are often more elaborate or detailed than in other problems.

unit_groups.name: Cyclotomic
unit_groups.subject: Algebra (Hufflepuff)
unit_groups.description: Roots of unity. Using $e^{i \theta} = \cos(\theta) + i \sin(\theta)$, and connecting them to trigonometry, etc. Features a ton of problems from Math Prize for Girls. Despite the name of the unit, cyclotomic polynomials themselves don't appear very often; the idea of roots of unity is much more prevalent.

unit_groups.name: Duluth
unit_groups.subject: Miscellaneous
unit_groups.description: A short end-of-year unit containing open problems I solved at the Duluth REU.

unit_groups.name: Dummy Unit
unit_groups.subject: Miscellaneous
unit_groups.description:
Not an actual unit; used internally as a canonical file for testing. If you unlock this unit, you will get a blank file. NOTHING TO SEE HERE MOVE ALONG.

More seriously you can use this to test the submission interface too if you are new to OTIS.

unit_groups.name: Elem Geo
unit_groups.subject: Geometry (Slytherin)
unit_groups.description: A unit featuring easy to medium geometry problems which can be solved using only the most basic tools: angle chasing, power of a point, homothety. It can be thought of as a follow-up to Part I of EGMO.

unit_groups.name: Entry Combo
unit_groups.subject: Combinatorics (Gryffindor)
unit_groups.description: A beginner combinatorics unit, meant to help get people oriented with typical proof styles for olympiad problems. Induction, recursion, invariants, and algorithms.

unit_groups.name: Equality
unit_groups.subject: Combinatorics (Gryffindor)
unit_groups.description: An important unit about taking advantage of the equality case in combinatorial problems in order to solve problems. Mandatory for newcomer students.

unit_groups.name: Euclid Alg
unit_groups.subject: Number Theory (Ravenclaw)
unit_groups.description: A unit about the idea that if $d$ divides $a$ and $b$, then $d$ divides any linear combination of $a$ and $b$. This intuition underlies Bezout's lemma, the Euclidean algorithm, and the division algorithm, as well as a technique which I privately call remainder bounding. One very good example of a problem of this feeling is SL 2016 N4 (sort of the crown example of remainder bounding).

unit_groups.name: Expected Value
unit_groups.subject: Combinatorics (Gryffindor)
unit_groups.description: Computational problems involving probability, expected value (in particular linearity of expectation), and Markov chains (processes which move from state to state). A good precursor to the Global unit.

unit_groups.name: Expon NT
unit_groups.subject: Number Theory (Ravenclaw)
unit_groups.description: Expressions of the form $a^n \pm 1$, the bread and butter of olympiad number theory. Mods and orders, Fermat's Christmas theorem, lifting the exponent.

unit_groups.name: Extremal Graph
unit_groups.subject: Combinatorics (Gryffindor)
unit_groups.description: A difficult unit on problems from extremal graph theory; finding graphs which maximize X under certain constructions. The most basic example is Turan's theorem, which maximizes the number of edges in a graph avoiding an r-clique. Global/local ideas as well as understanding of equality cases feature prominently. Most of the examples in this unit are more challenging.

unit_groups.name: Forest of Wandering (Lv 35)
unit_groups.subject: Secret
unit_groups.description: Secret unit unlocked that appears at level 35.

unit_groups.name: Formulas
unit_groups.subject: Algebra (Hufflepuff)
unit_groups.description: Based on Yang Liu's class "Write Down Formulas" at MOP 2018. This unit consists of problems which involve manipulations of fairly involved formulas, such as combinatorial recursions or number theoretic power sums. Despite officially being algebra, there are just as many (maybe more) problems that would be classified as C or N.

unit_groups.name: Free
unit_groups.subject: Combinatorics (Gryffindor)
unit_groups.description: Problems for which you have a lot of room to make decisions; a lot of the problems in this unit are constructions, for example. You will feel like you are inventing mathematics, rather than discovering it (in contrast to the Rigid unit).

unit_groups.name: Func Eqn
unit_groups.subject: Functional Equations
unit_groups.description: Functional equations, I guess.

unit_groups.name: G6 Summit
unit_groups.subject: Geometry (Slytherin)
unit_groups.description: A challenging geometry unit to conclude the year, with difficult problems reviewing everything that has appeared earlier.

unit_groups.name: Gen Func
unit_groups.subject: Algebra (Hufflepuff)
unit_groups.description: Generating functions and their use in algebraic contexts (computing sums, featuring the so-called snake oil method). Of course, some combinatorial problems included as well.

unit_groups.name: Global
unit_groups.subject: Combinatorics (Gryffindor)
unit_groups.description: Linearity of expectation, switching the order of summation, what's often called pigeonhole principle, counting in two ways, ... turns out they're actually all more or less the same idea.

unit_groups.name: Global and Local
unit_groups.subject: Combinatorics (Gryffindor)
unit_groups.description: An accelerated version of both the global and local units (both done at once).

unit_groups.name: Graph Theory
unit_groups.subject: Combinatorics (Gryffindor)
unit_groups.description: Combinatorics practice with graphs. The B version of the unit is aimed at students with no previous exposure to graph theory; the harder versions assume prior experience.

unit_groups.name: Grids
unit_groups.subject: Combinatorics (Gryffindor)
unit_groups.description: A fun but difficult unit on combinatorics problems involving rectangular grids.

unit_groups.name: Grinding
unit_groups.subject: Miscellaneous
unit_groups.description: The worst olympiad problems you've ever seen. The name is a reference to the video game term in which you do the same thing over and over.

unit_groups.name: Groups, Rings, and Fields
unit_groups.subject: Higher Math
unit_groups.description: A first introduction to the language of group theory, rings, and fields. One of the two key gateway units for higher math.

unit_groups.name: Hall
unit_groups.subject: Combinatorics (Gryffindor)
unit_groups.description: Hall's marriage lemma.

unit_groups.name: Hard Ineq
unit_groups.subject: Algebra (Hufflepuff)
unit_groups.description: A unit featuring some of the hardest gems from the golden age of inequalities.

unit_groups.name: Harmonic
unit_groups.subject: Geometry (Slytherin)
unit_groups.description: Your friendly projective geometry unit. Harmonic bundles, poles and polars, and so on. A follow-up to Chapter 9 of EGMO.

unit_groups.name: Heavy NT
unit_groups.subject: Number Theory (Ravenclaw)
unit_groups.description: Heavy machinery in number theory: Vieta jumping, quadratic reciprocity, and some big-name theorems you may or may not have heard of.

unit_groups.name: Hill of Memories (Lv 47)
unit_groups.subject: Secret
unit_groups.description: Secret unit that appears at level 47.

unit_groups.name: Homography
unit_groups.subject: Geometry (Slytherin)
unit_groups.description: A less friendly and more abstract projective geometry unit, with an emphasis on projective transformations. Most of the theorems will be stated with respect to an arbitrary conic rather than a circle. Expect lots of Pascal. Includes Desargues involution theorem (DIT) and its dual (DDIT).

unit_groups.name: Hybrid Geo
unit_groups.subject: Geometry (Slytherin)
unit_groups.description: Grab-bag of geometry problems that feature some algebra, combinatorics, or number theory. As examples, this includes geometric inequalities, combinatorial geometry, and problems involving integer distances or lattice points.

unit_groups.name: Hyperbola
unit_groups.subject: Geometry (Slytherin)
unit_groups.description: A silly (but difficult) unit on the theory of rectangular circumhyperbolas and the Poncelet point. For fun, if you really like hardcore projective geometry.

unit_groups.name: Induction & Recursion I
unit_groups.subject: Combinatorics (Gryffindor)
unit_groups.description: Problems which really use induction and recursion in a substantial way, i.e. the main idea of the problem really is about how (or whether) to induct. Features some AIME-style recursion calculations.

unit_groups.name: Induction & Recursion II
unit_groups.subject: Combinatorics (Gryffindor)
unit_groups.description: This is a duplicate of Induction I, but more difficult and with different artwork.

unit_groups.name: Ineq Basic
unit_groups.subject: Algebra (Hufflepuff)
unit_groups.description: The basics of inequalities: AM-GM, homogenization, Cauchy/Holder.

unit_groups.name: Ineq Func
unit_groups.subject: Algebra (Hufflepuff)
unit_groups.description: Techniques for inequalities of the form $f(x_1) + ... + f(x_n)$. Jensen, Karamata, tangent line trick. The $n-1$ equal value principle is deferred to Ineq Standard.

unit_groups.name: Ineq Standard
unit_groups.subject: Algebra (Hufflepuff)
unit_groups.description: Inequalities which can be approached using all the standard methods. This is sort of a combination of Ineq Basic and Ineq Func.

unit_groups.name: Insect Land (Lv 61)
unit_groups.subject: Secret
unit_groups.description: Secret unit that appears at level 61.

unit_groups.name: Int Polynom
unit_groups.subject: Number Theory (Ravenclaw)
unit_groups.description: A theoretical unit on the algebra and number theory of polynomials over $\mathbb Z$, bordering into some algebraic number theory and Galois theory. Algebraic integers and irreducible polynomials feature prominently in this unit. The number theory in this unit goes deeper than that used in the Irreducible unit.

unit_groups.name: Integration Bee
unit_groups.subject: Algebra (Hufflepuff)
unit_groups.description: Was your calculus class too easy for you? Do you want to stare at random artificial expressions having no idea how to find their antiderivative? If so, this unit is for you! Enjoy a student-contributed guest unit featuring problems from integration bees from MIT and other places.

unit_groups.name: Intermediate Mix
unit_groups.subject: Combinatorics (Gryffindor)
unit_groups.description: Mixed combinatorics practice at the IMO 2/5 level.

unit_groups.name: Inversion and Spiral
unit_groups.subject: Geometry (Slytherin)
unit_groups.description: A harder follow-up unit to chapters 8 and 10 of EGMO.

unit_groups.name: Invert
unit_groups.subject: Geometry (Slytherin)
unit_groups.description: Inversion in olympiad geometry, following up chapter 8 of EGMO.

unit_groups.name: IOI
unit_groups.subject: Combinatorics (Gryffindor)
unit_groups.description: Algorithmic problems which involve showing that it is possible to achieve some task (rather than finding invariants or proving impossibility). Features selected problems from the IOI, so some CS background is helpful but not necessary.

unit_groups.name: Irreducible
unit_groups.subject: Algebra (Hufflepuff)
unit_groups.description: Problems about showing polynomials are irreducible; these are rare in olympiads, but give you a lot of good intuition about how $\mathbb{Z}[x]$ polynomials behave. Techniques that appear include working in $\mathbb{F}_p[x]$, looking at the magnitude of complex roots a la Rouche, and other ad-hoc tricks. More olympiad algebra than the integer polynomials unit.

unit_groups.name: Linear Algebra
unit_groups.subject: Combinatorics (Gryffindor)
unit_groups.description: Problems using linear algebra (rather than problems about linear algebra). Most of the problems here are combinatorial in nature as a result, and there is a mix of linear algebra over $\mathbb R$ and linear algebra over $\mathbb{F}_2$.

unit_groups.name: Linear Power
unit_groups.subject: Geometry (Slytherin)
unit_groups.description: This unit revolves around two particular techniques: linearity of a difference of power of a point and the forgotten coaxiality lemma. This is used to compare powers of a point with respect to two different circles, particularly showing they are equal.

unit_groups.name: Local
unit_groups.subject: Combinatorics (Gryffindor)
unit_groups.description: In contrast to the global unit, this unit is about problems starting from somewhere and perturbing it by a little bit. For example, in a greedy algorithm, if I want a set $S$ of size at least 100 with a certain property, I can imagine starting with $S$ empty and then grabbing things to add to $S$ while trying to avoid bad-ness (whatever that means for the current problem). It's then enough to prove I don't get stuck at any point. Most of the problems in this unit will have a similar algorithmic feeling.

unit_groups.name: Misc NT
unit_groups.subject: Number Theory (Ravenclaw)
unit_groups.description: Miscellaneous number theory problems that didn't fit well in other units.

unit_groups.name: Mod Arith 1: Proofs
unit_groups.subject: Number Theory (Ravenclaw)
unit_groups.description:
One of two beginner units in number theory focusing on modular arithmetic.
Emphasis on basic proof-based problems, starting with simple Diophantine equations that can be solved by taking moduluses and factoring.
The two Modular Arithmetic units can be done in either order.

unit_groups.name: Mod Arith 2: Computation
unit_groups.subject: Number Theory (Ravenclaw)
unit_groups.description:
One of two beginner units in number theory focusing on modular arithmetic.
Emphasis on concrete calculation in problems which ultimately ask for a numerical answer.
The two Modular Arithmetic units can be done in either order.

unit_groups.name: Monster FE
unit_groups.subject: Functional Equations
unit_groups.description: The functional equations that don't bore me, because the solution isn't just $f(x) = x$ anymore! This follows up the Monsters handout on my website.

unit_groups.name: Moving Points
unit_groups.subject: Geometry (Slytherin)
unit_groups.description: A technique involving animating points and considering resulting projective maps. This lecture was contributed by Anant Mudgal.

unit_groups.name: Multivariable Calculus
unit_groups.subject: Higher Math
unit_groups.description: Multivariable calculus as taught at MIT in fall 2024, based on the LAMV textbook written by Evan. Covers multivariable differentiation, multivariable integration, and special cases of the general Stokes theorem for dimension up to 3. Requires only a high school background up to single-variable calculus. Half the problems are exercises from my book and the other half are past Putnam problems.

unit_groups.name: Mystery
unit_groups.subject: Combinatorics (Gryffindor)
unit_groups.description: This one's a secret. It's a bit weird, but for this unit to work I have to start by not telling you what it's about.

unit_groups.name: N7 Summit
unit_groups.subject: Number Theory (Ravenclaw)
unit_groups.description: A challenging number theory unit to conclude the year, with difficult problems reviewing everything that has appeared earlier.

unit_groups.name: NT Construct
unit_groups.subject: Number Theory (Ravenclaw)
unit_groups.description: Construction problems in number theory. In some ways it's like the free unit because you get to make some decisions, but in other ways Z has a lot of structure that you might know things about, and you'll have to balance these two intuitions.

unit_groups.name: Orders
unit_groups.subject: Number Theory (Ravenclaw)
unit_groups.description: Orders modulo a prime, at the border of AIME and USA(J)MO but leaning a lot more towards the latter. Intended as an introduction into olympiad number theory.

unit_groups.name: Prime Exponents
unit_groups.subject: Number Theory (Ravenclaw)
unit_groups.description: The use of $\nu_p$ in handling olympiad problems.

unit_groups.name: Process
unit_groups.subject: Combinatorics (Gryffindor)
unit_groups.description: This is about staring at a moving process (e.g. windmill) and trying to understand what is going on. (The most common thing people say here is monovariants or invariants, but that's only one example of a way you can understand a process.) In a lot of ways it's like the Rigid unit, except your data is way less concrete, and in some cases unobtainable, so you'll be applying the same intuition in a more hostile environment.

unit_groups.name: Putnam Analysis
unit_groups.subject: Algebra (Hufflepuff)
unit_groups.description: An extension of the Sums unit --- rather than just swapping infinite sums, we now get to enjoy swapping infinite integrals as well.

unit_groups.name: Real Polynom
unit_groups.subject: Algebra (Hufflepuff)
unit_groups.description: General polynomials unit, maintaining some distance from integer polynomials (though still overlapping slightly). Includes Vieta/Newton, multivariable polynomials, Lagrange interpolation, size arguments, differentiation.

unit_groups.name: Real-to-Real Func Eqn
unit_groups.subject: Functional Equations
unit_groups.description: A really standard cookie-cutter unit with functional equations over real numbers or positive real numbers. Generally more dry than the other functional equational units, so only recommended if you really want additional FE practice or really enjoy FE's.

unit_groups.name: Rigid
unit_groups.subject: Combinatorics (Gryffindor)
unit_groups.description: This is one of my favorite units. It's about problems which involve taking a fixed structure, and trying to figure out as much as you can about it --- the task the problem asks you to actually prove becomes unimportant, almost like an answer extraction at the end. Rigid problems often have so few degrees of freedom that a lot of what you'll be doing is writing down a lot of concrete examples, and then trying to figure out what they have in common. You will feel like you are discovering mathematics, rather than inventing it (in contrast to the Free unit).

unit_groups.name: Russian Combo
unit_groups.subject: Combinatorics (Gryffindor)
unit_groups.description: A fun unit involving combinatorics problems from Russia.

unit_groups.name: Size in NT
unit_groups.subject: Number Theory (Ravenclaw)
unit_groups.description: Using size as a way to handle number theory conditions, for example taking sufficiently large primes. On the border between olympiad algebra and olympiad number theory.

unit_groups.name: Spiral
unit_groups.subject: Geometry (Slytherin)
unit_groups.description: Spiral similarity and Miquel points, following up chapter 10 of EGMO.

unit_groups.name: Sums
unit_groups.subject: Algebra (Hufflepuff)
unit_groups.description: Practice with manipulating sums, and in particular switching the order of summation (or integration). Features generating functions and Snake Oil as well. The Gen Func unit is an optional prerequisite.

unit_groups.name: Super Bary
unit_groups.subject: Geometry (Slytherin)
unit_groups.description: A more difficult version of the barycentric coordinates unit.

unit_groups.name: Super Complex
unit_groups.subject: Geometry (Slytherin)
unit_groups.description: A more difficult version of the complex numbers unit.

unit_groups.name: Super NT
unit_groups.subject: Number Theory (Ravenclaw)
unit_groups.description: Number theory practice for experts, combining problems from Exp and Heavy NT as well as some other sources.

unit_groups.name: Symm Polynom
unit_groups.subject: Algebra (Hufflepuff)
unit_groups.description: Vieta formulas, Newton sums, and the fundamental theorem of symmetric polynomials. Involves some computational problems. In my opinion, this is probably the easiest unit.

unit_groups.name: Topology and Real Analysis
unit_groups.subject: Higher Math
unit_groups.description: A quick introduction to important notions like continuity, compactness, topological spaces, etc. One of two key gateway units for higher math.

unit_groups.name: Tricky Ineq
unit_groups.subject: Algebra (Hufflepuff)
unit_groups.description: Harder inequality problems that don't succumb to the standard methods: it takes some more ingenuity to figure out how to approach these.

unit_groups.name: Trig and Lengths
unit_groups.subject: Geometry (Slytherin)
unit_groups.description: The trig-bash unit. Several traditional-style geometry problems that are meant to be solved by chasing lengths and ratios, with the aid of trigonometric techniques.

unit_groups.name: Weird Geo
unit_groups.subject: Geometry (Slytherin)
unit_groups.description: Those weird geometry problems that involve pentagons and hexagons and whatnot (see USAMO 2011/3 for example). Careful use of complex numbers and counting degrees of freedom are important for this unit.

unit_groups.name: Wrapped Func Eqn
unit_groups.subject: Functional Equations
unit_groups.description: On real-valued functional equations in which all variables are "wrapped" by the function in some way.
